biotech.title: Neural Pulse
biotech.description: Clinical-style EEG review and neural signal playground for exploring demo or uploaded EEG-style data.
biotech.longDescription: Neural Pulse is the EEG flagship: a clinical-style review playground for demo and uploaded EEG-style data, with playback, band modes, referencing, montage controls, smoothing, and a roadmap toward artifact classification and exportable sessions.
biotech.whyBuilt: I wanted a way to make neural data feel less abstract. A lot of interesting patterns only become obvious once the signal becomes something you can actually interact with.
biotech.problem: Most neural data tools feel either too academic, too static, or too infrastructure-heavy for fast exploratory review.
biotech.approach: Use browser-native signal visualisation and client-side processing to make EEG inspection, filtering, and interpretation feel immediate and private.
whatWorksNow: Demo EEG datasets covering alpha, beta, theta, and spike-heavy activity
Playback controls with scrubbing and speed control
Band modes: delta, theta, alpha, beta, and gamma
whatILearned: Signal tools need clarity more than complexity
Client-side privacy is a strong framing advantage
Scientific interfaces work better when they feel exploratory rather than dashboard-like
biotech.limitations: Current scope is still prototype-level and relies on demo or uploaded data rather than a broader validated clinical workflow.
biotech.nextSteps: Add richer artifact classification, channel quality scoring, saved review presets, and shareable session exports.
credibility: Scientific interface rather than generic dashboard
highlights: EEG review with privacy-aware client-side workflows
biotech.privacyNote: Designed around client-side exploration so uploaded neural data can stay local to the browser.

biotech.title: BioBody Insights
biotech.description: Interactive whole-body systems biology atlas for exploring how genes, pathways, tissues, and metabolic flux connect across disease programs.
biotech.longDescription: BioBody Insights maps genes, pathways, tissue effects, metabolic flux, and disease programs onto an anatomical body interface so complex systems biology can be explored as a spatial story rather than a disconnected table.
biotech.whyBuilt: A lot of biological information becomes hard to discuss once it leaves the lab notebook and turns into disconnected slides or pathway screenshots.
biotech.problem: Cross-functional teams often struggle to reason through pathway or tissue-level biology when the information is buried in static diagrams and disconnected notes.
biotech.approach: Use a body-mapped interface to anchor pathway and programme exploration in a more intuitive scientific narrative.
whatILearned: Biology interfaces benefit from spatial anchors
Interpretability matters more than raw density
Scientific storytelling can be a product skill
biotech.limitations: It is a visual interpretation layer, not a full knowledgebase or validated biomedical decision-support system.
biotech.nextSteps: Add richer disease programs, search across genes/pathways/tissues, evidence and confidence metadata, import support for omics or assay data, and exportable reports.

biotech.title: Gene Expression Profiling
biotech.description: Client-side tool concept for exploring gene expression patterns, fold-change, clustering, and disease-linked signals.
biotech.longDescription: A browser-first concept for comparing expression patterns, surfacing potential therapeutic targets, and turning raw expression tables into interpretable heatmaps, fold-change views, and target-prioritisation narratives.
biotech.whyBuilt: Gene expression work is powerful but often locked behind tooling that feels inaccessible unless you already live inside analysis environments.
biotech.problem: Expression datasets are often difficult to explore quickly when analysis requires a heavy local stack or fragmented tooling.
biotech.approach: Translate common exploratory workflows into a client-side concept built around upload, comparison, filtering, and visual prioritisation.
whatILearned: The hard part is interpretability, not just charting
Biological workflows need careful framing to avoid overclaiming
Privacy-aware analysis concepts are a useful differentiator
biotech.limitations: This is still a concept rather than a validated analysis environment with robust statistical pipelines.
biotech.nextSteps: Add data quality checks, stronger comparison modes, clustering controls, and biologically meaningful prioritisation views.
biotech.privacyNote: Planned as a local-first exploration tool for sensitive biological datasets.

biotech.title: Bioinformatics Sequence Alignment Tool
biotech.description: DNA, RNA, and protein sequence comparison tool for aligning sequences and highlighting mutations.
biotech.longDescription: A planned local analysis tool for comparing sequences, annotating regions of interest, and making mutation differences easier to inspect in a visual browser-based workflow.

biotech.title: Microarray Data Processor
biotech.description: Microarray-style upload and analysis tool for normalisation, filtering, comparison, and visualisation.
biotech.longDescription: A planned analysis workspace for normalising matrix-style biological datasets, filtering signal, and comparing groups through clustering and heatmap-driven exploration.

biotech.title: Virtual Lab Simulator
biotech.description: Interactive simulation of PCR, qPCR, LAMP, solution preparation, and GMP-style workflow decisions.
biotech.longDescription: A training-oriented simulator for lab and manufacturing-adjacent workflows, combining PCR, qPCR, LAMP, solution preparation, documentation checkpoints, and GMP-style decision points that reflect real scientific practice.
biotech.whyBuilt: A lot of scientific training only becomes real once someone is physically in the workflow. I wanted to explore whether some of that understanding could be made interactive earlier.
biotech.problem: Scientific training tools rarely reflect the procedural and documentation mindset that real GMP or assay workflows require.
biotech.approach: Build an interactive simulator around practical lab steps, checkpoints, and consequences rather than abstract educational content alone.
whatWorksNow: Strong concept fit with real lab practice
Good bridge between scientific education and interface design
whatILearned: Process tools need believable constraints
Documentation can be part of the interaction, not an afterthought
Scientific training is a surprisingly rich design space
biotech.limitations: Still conceptual and not yet tied to a validated curriculum or full workflow library.
biotech.nextSteps: Prototype one concrete training path first, likely PCR/qPCR or a GMP documentation flow, before expanding.
highlights: Scientific training concept grounded in real lab workflows
Could bridge education, SOP thinking, and interactive tooling

biotech.title: 3D Tissue Engineering Visualization
biotech.description: Visual exploration of scaffolds, biomaterials, cell growth, and regenerative medicine concepts.
biotech.longDescription: A regenerative-medicine inspired concept for visualising scaffold structures, biomaterial choices, and tissue growth ideas in a way that connects scientific education with interactive 3D explanation.
biotech.whyBuilt: My MSc work left me with a lasting interest in how regenerative medicine concepts are explained, especially when the science is spatial and hard to picture.
biotech.problem: Regenerative medicine concepts are hard to communicate when spatial scaffold behaviour and biomaterial tradeoffs stay abstract.
biotech.approach: Use interactive 3D-oriented explanation to make scaffold, growth, and material choices easier to grasp.
whatWorksNow: Good thematic fit with academic background
Strong visual direction for future prototype work
whatILearned: Some scientific ideas need spatial explanation first
3D is most useful when it clarifies tradeoffs, not just for style
biotech.limitations: Conceptual only, with no real simulation fidelity yet.
biotech.nextSteps: Prototype a single scaffold-comparison flow and one simplified growth visualisation mode.

biotech.title: Drug Interaction Predictor
biotech.description: Educational client-side concept for exploring known drug interactions and risk categories.
biotech.longDescription: A planned educational lookup and interpretation tool for drug interactions, with emphasis on risk explanation and accessible pharmacology-oriented presentation.

biotech.title: Disease Outbreak Prediction Model
biotech.description: Exploratory visual model showing how population density, travel, climate, and geography can influence outbreak risk.
biotech.longDescription: A public-health oriented visual model concept for simulating outbreak conditions and communicating how multiple environmental and societal variables interact over time.

biotech.title: Environmental Impact Calculator
biotech.description: Calculator for estimating the environmental footprint of biotech and lab workflows.
biotech.longDescription: A sustainability concept for comparing consumables, energy use, waste, and cold-chain decisions across lab and biotech process scenarios.

personal.title: Live Vision Model Lab
personal.description: Installable browser vision playground for real-time webcam, image, and video inference using switchable client-side models.
personal.longDescription: Live Vision Model Lab is a standalone installable browser playground for real-time webcam, image, and video inference with switchable TensorFlow.js and face-api.js models, exportable inference payloads, local face-api weights, and a privacy-first local inference stance.
personal.whyBuilt: I wanted to see how far useful computer vision could go in the browser without sending a camera feed to an external service.
personal.problem: Most quick vision demos either send data to a backend or feel too toy-like to evaluate seriously.
personal.approach: Keep the inference in-browser and focus on direct interaction so model behaviour is legible without sacrificing privacy.
whatWorksNow: Webcam, image upload, and video upload modes
Export latest inference payload to JSON/CSV
Models: COCO-SSD, MobileNet, Handpose, face detection, face expression
whatILearned: Privacy-first positioning matters
Client-side constraints force better prioritisation
Small demos can still feel like real products
personal.limitations: Model scope and mobile performance still limit the breadth of tasks that feel robust.
personal.nextSteps: Polish mobile performance, clarify the model comparison UI, and improve privacy/offline messaging.
highlights: Useful as a standalone technical demo rather than an embedded portfolio widget
personal.privacyNote: Camera and inference workflows are intended to stay in-browser where possible.

personal.title: Pokemon Card Scanner / Deck Valuation Tool
personal.description: Fun computer-vision/OCR concept for identifying Pokemon cards and estimating collection/deck value.
personal.longDescription: A playful but credible scanning and lookup concept for recognising cards from uploads, matching them to known inventory, and estimating collection or deck-level value through a cleaner interface.
personal.whyBuilt: Part nostalgia, part curiosity about whether a playful recognition tool could still be designed with the same seriousness as a scientific workflow.
personal.problem: Collectors often use fragmented tools that handle scanning, matching, and valuation poorly as one workflow.
personal.approach: Design a cleaner recognition-to-valuation flow that could make deck-level review feel more usable and fun.
whatWorksNow: Good testbed for image-recognition UI thinking
whatILearned: Playful projects still benefit from rigorous product framing
Consumer tooling can sharpen interaction design instincts
personal.limitations: Currently a concept and not yet backed by a real pricing or card-matching dataset.
personal.nextSteps: Prototype upload flow, OCR pass, and placeholder matching before any live price integrations.

personal.title: Audio-Reactive Visualizer
personal.description: Creative browser visualiser driven by microphone or uploaded audio.
personal.longDescription: A creative coding concept using browser audio analysis to drive generative or signal-reactive visuals that could be shared or captured as outputs.
personal.whyBuilt: It is a lighter way to explore the same idea that runs through a lot of the site: invisible signals become more interesting once they are made visual.
whatWorksNow: Strong conceptual connection to signal work
Good candidate for a future browser prototype
whatILearned: Creative tools can still reflect scientific instincts
Signal visualisation is a useful bridge between serious and playful work

personal.title: Neural Network Playground
personal.description: Educational interactive neural network toy for experimenting with layers and simple classification tasks.
personal.longDescription: A lightweight educational playground for adjusting simple model parameters and visualising how training feedback changes on toy datasets.
personal.whyBuilt: Mostly curiosity. I like tools that make abstract processes easier to see without pretending to be more serious than they are.
whatWorksNow: Useful as a lightweight concept rather than a core portfolio claim
whatILearned: Clarity beats sophistication in teaching tools
Some ideas are worth keeping playful

media.title: Monochrome Lab Portrait
media.description: A quiet portrait study with minimal palette and directional light.

media.title: Field Notes in Motion
media.description: A short-form reel style sequence balancing travel atmosphere with research textures.

media.title: Coastal Editorial Frame
media.description: Editorial still with cinematic color treatment and spatial negative space.

media.description: Textural frames from biotech experimentation and instrumentation details.
